name: Yonaguni Monument
description: The Yonaguni Monument, also known as Yonaguni Island Submarine Ruins, is a submerged rock formation off the coast of Yonaguni, Japan. Proponents suggest it's a deliberately constructed, ancient pyramid or city, possibly dating back over 10,000 years and predating known civilizations. They point to its apparent geometric features, such as flat platforms, right angles, steps, and what appears to be a carved face, as evidence of artificial construction. Masaaki Kimura, a marine geologist, is a key proponent. Mainstream geology attributes the formation to natural geological processes, specifically wave erosion and tectonic activity on sandstone. Critics argue that the features, while appearing geometric, are consistent with naturally fractured rock formations. They cite the absence of mortar, tools, or artifacts indicative of human construction in the immediate area. The debate revolves around interpreting the existing geological evidence and whether natural processes can fully account for the monument's features.
subtitle: Submerged rock formation or ancient city?

claims.claim: The monument is a man-made structure.
claims.evidence: Geometric shapes, flat platforms, right angles, steps, carved face.
claims.criticism: Natural geological processes can create similar features through erosion and fracturing.

claims.claim: The monument dates back over 10,000 years.
claims.evidence: Proponents estimate age based on sea level changes since the last ice age.
claims.criticism: Dating is based on circumstantial evidence; no direct dating of the structure itself exists.

claims.claim: The monument is evidence of a lost civilization.
claims.evidence: Complex construction suggests advanced planning and engineering skills.
claims.criticism: Lack of associated artifacts or evidence of a civilization in the area supports natural formation hypothesis.

keyFigures.name: Masaaki Kimura
keyFigures.role: Marine geologist advocating for artificial origin

keyFigures.name: Robert Schoch
keyFigures.role: Geologist who initially supported artificial origin, then became more cautious

keyFigures.name: Patrick D. Nunn
keyFigures.role: Professor of Geography who supports the natural formation hypothesis

mythologyConnections: Connections to legends of lost continents like Mu or Lemuria
Possible inspiration for local Ryukyuan legends of underwater cities

timeline.event: The Yonaguni Monument is discovered by Kihachiro Aratake, a local diver.
Masaaki Kimura begins extensive research and advocates for the artificial origin theory.
Debate continues between proponents of artificial and natural origin theories.

sources.author: Nunn, P.D.
sources.work: Environment, People, and MEGA-Disasters in Prehistory
sources.title: Nunn, P.D.

sources.author: Hancock, G.
sources.work: Underworld: The Mysterious Origins of Civilization
sources.title: Hancock, G.
